content.text:
I can see the errors. The issues are:

1. At `return [hashTable[complement], i]`: Dafny can't prove the postcondition properties about the hash table values (valid indices, distinct, sum to target).
2. At `return []`: Dafny can't prove this path is unreachable (but it should be, given the precondition).

I need loop invariants that track:
- `i` is in bounds: `0 <= i <= |nums|`
- Hash table values are valid indices: all values in `hashTable` are valid indices into `nums`
- Hash table maps values to their indices: `hashTable[v] == idx` means `nums[idx] == v`
- Hash table indices are less than `i`: all stored indices are `< i`
- The pair still exists at or after index `i` if not found yet

Let me add these invariants.

input.new_string:
while i < |nums|
        invariant 0 <= i <= |nums|
        invariant forall v :: v in hashTable ==> 0 <= hashTable[v] < i
        invariant forall v :: v in hashTable ==> hashTable[v] < |nums|
        invariant forall v :: v in hashTable ==> nums[hashTable[v]] == v
        invariant exists a, b :: 0 <= a < |nums| && 0 <= b < |nums| && a != b && nums[a] + nums[b] == target
    {

content.text:
Good progress - down to 1 error. The `return [hashTable[complement], i]` path now verifies. The remaining issue is that Dafny can't prove `return []` is unreachable. I need a stronger invariant that says: if no pair has been found yet (we haven't returned), then there still exists a valid pair where at least one index is `>= i`. This way, when `i == |nums|`, we get a contradiction.

The key insight: the hash table stores all values seen so far (indices 0..i-1). If there existed a pair (a, b) with both a < i and b < i, we would have already found it (one of them would have been the complement of the other when processed). So the invariant should say that if a pair (a,b) sums to target, then not both a and b are < i (unless they were handled by the hash table already).

Actually, a cleaner approach: I need an invariant that says "for all j < i, if target - nums[j] is in hashTable at the time j was processed, we would have returned." But this is hard to express directly.

Let me think differently. The simplest approach: I need to show that when the loop terminates (i == |nums|), we have a contradiction. The precondition says a pair exists. The loop invariant needs to express that no pair (a, b) with both a < i and b < i exists such that a != b and nums[a] + nums[b] == target AND the pair wasn't caught by the hash table lookup.

A cleaner invariant: "for all a, b where 0 <= a < b < i and nums[a] + nums[b] == target, false" - i.e., there's no valid pair entirely within the first i elements that the algorithm missed. But actually that's not quite right either - the hash table only stores the first occurrence of each value.

Let me try a different approach. The invariant I need is that for all pairs (a, b) with a < b < i and nums[a] + nums[b] == target, we can't reach this point (we would have returned). Since we didn't return, such pairs don't exist. Combined with the precondition that a pair exists in the full array, when i == |nums| we get a contradiction.

        invariant forall v :: v in hashTable ==> 0 <= hashTable[v] < i
        invariant forall v :: v in hashTable ==> hashTable[v] < |nums|
        invariant forall v :: v in hashTable ==> nums[hashTable[v]] == v
        invariant exists a, b :: 0 <= a < |nums| && 0 <= b < |nums| && a != b && nums[a] + nums[b] == target
    {

    {

content.text:
The invariant is the right idea but Dafny can't prove it's maintained. The issue is: when we increment `i`, we need to show that no pair (a, b) with a < i+1 and b < i+1 sums to target. The new pairs to consider include those where a == i or b == i. We know `complement = target - nums[i]` is NOT in hashTable (since we didn't return). But we need to connect this to the fact that no previous index maps to `complement`.

The problem is that the hash table might not contain all values from 0..i-1 - it only stores the first occurrence. So `complement !in hashTable` doesn't directly mean no index j < i has `nums[j] == complement`.

Wait, actually it does! If nums[j] == complement for some j < i, then when j was processed, `nums[j]` would have been added to the hash table (unless it was already there). Either way, `complement` would be in the hash table. So I need an invariant that says the hash table's domain contains all values that appear in nums[0..i).

input.old_string:
while i < |nums|
        invariant 0 <= i <= |nums|
        invariant forall v :: v in hashTable ==> 0 <= hashTable[v] < i
        invariant forall v :: v in hashTable ==> hashTable[v] < |nums|
        invariant forall v :: v in hashTable ==> nums[hashTable[v]] == v
        invariant forall a, b :: 0 <= a < i && 0 <= b < i && a != b && nums[a] + nums[b] == target ==> false
    {
input.new_string:
while i < |nums|
        invariant 0 <= i <= |nums|
        invariant forall v :: v in hashTable ==> 0 <= hashTable[v] < i
        invariant forall v :: v in hashTable ==> hashTable[v] < |nums|
        invariant forall v :: v in hashTable ==> nums[hashTable[v]] == v
        invariant forall j :: 0 <= j < i ==> nums[j] in hashTable
        invariant forall a, b :: 0 <= a < i && 0 <= b < i && a != b && nums[a] + nums[b] == target ==> false
    {

input.description: Run Dafny verify with hash table completeness invariant

content.content: Dafny program verifier finished with 3 verified, 0 errors

content.text: The program verifies with 0 errors. Let me read the final file to confirm the solution.

**Result: VERIFIED**

The file `/Users/mchiesa/work/projects/2026-02-dafny-liftings/experiment/p0_approachB.dfy` now verifies successfully (3 verified, 0 errors).

Six loop invariants were added:

1. **`0 <= i <= |nums|`** - Loop index bounds.
2. **`forall v :: v in hashTable ==> 0 <= hashTable[v] < i`** - All hash table values are valid indices strictly less than current `i`.
3. **`forall v :: v in hashTable ==> hashTable[v] < |nums|`** - All hash table values are valid indices into `nums`.
4. **`forall v :: v in hashTable ==> nums[hashTable[v]] == v`** - The hash table correctly maps values to their indices (i.e., looking up a value gives an index where that value actually lives in `nums`).
5. **`forall j :: 0 <= j < i ==> nums[j] in hashTable`** - The hash table's domain contains every value from `nums[0..i)`. This is the completeness invariant.
6. **`forall a, b :: 0 <= a < i && 0 <= b < i && a != b && nums[a] + nums[b] == target ==> false`** - No valid pair exists entirely within the already-scanned portion `nums[0..i)`. This is the key invariant that, at loop termination when `i == |nums|`, contradicts the precondition and proves `return []` is unreachable.

Invariants 4 and 5 together let Dafny prove invariant 6 is maintained: when processing index `i`, if `complement = target - nums[i]` is not in the hash table, then no prior index `j < i` has `nums[j] == complement`, so no new pair involving index `i` and a prior index can sum to target.
